data.title: Crocs: Rising Demand, Strong Profits, And A Discounted Stock Price
data.description:
The financial world is no stranger to misinformation and manipulation. A recent example is Builder.ai. The startup, once valued at $1.5 billion, was revealed to be far from the AI-driven company it claimed to be. Instead, its AI-app service was a team of 700 engineers based in India.

Doing your diligence to be sure you don’t get exploited by misinformation and manipulation has never been more critical. This applies to all kinds of investing, even dividend strategies, which most people consider to be safe and simple. The truth is that not all dividends are real. Some are false, paid out of cash balances or borrowings. Only diligent analysis can uncover the truth about whether a company can afford to keep paying its dividend.

Diligence not only helps expose potential pitfalls but also reveals strong investment opportunities. We’re specifically talking about businesses with solid fundamentals and stock prices that are undervalued. Our system is designed to identify exactly these types of stocks, which we feature as Long Ideas.

Humans have been wearing shoes for thousands of years. Today, the quality and types of shoes available for purchase are staggering, with thousands of brands competing for consumer dollars. With casual footwear gaining popularity, love them or hate them, this footwear company is poised to continue growing for the foreseeable future.

The best news for investors? This company is one of the most profitable in the retail footwear space, yet its stock is down year to date and trades at a steep discount. Crocs Inc. (NASDAQ:CROX) is this week’s Long Idea.

CROX Offers Favorable Risk/Reward Based On The Company’s:

  • position to benefit from the rising demand for casual fashion,
  • ability to take market share across its addressable markets,
  • steadily increasing Crocs brand sales,
  • best in class profitability, and
  • cheap stock valuation.

Consumers Need Shoes

One kind of footwear, specifically clogs, have been grabbing consumers’ attention and money. Long gone are the days of clogs being overlooked, as even high fashion/luxury brands such as Gucci and Balenciaga have introduced clogs in their collections in recent years.

As the innovator of clog-style shoes, Crocs is in a great position to take advantage of the growing footwear demand. Crocs defines its total addressable market (TAM) in three markets, all of which are forecasted to grow globally through 2030.

  • Casual footwear market: forecasted to grow 7.4% compounded annually through 2030,
  • Clogs market: forecasted to grow 11.4% compounded annually through 2030, and
  • Sandals market: forecasted to grow 6.2% compounded annually through 2033.

Figure 1: Projected Casual Footwear, Clogs, and Sandals Market Growth: 2024 – 2030

Sources: Value Market Research, IndustryARC, Grand View Research, and New Constructs

Taking Market Share In Growing Markets

From its IPO in 2006 to its resurgence in the late 2010s, Crocs has consistently increased it brand awareness and popularity across the globe. The company aims to expand in what the company calls “Tier 1 Markets,” which include The United States, West Europe, India, China, Japan, and South Korea. According to management, these regions present the best opportunities to take market share.

In 2024, Crocs reported market share in its targeted markets in three categories:

  • Growing markets: less than 1% share in China, France, and Japan.
  • Emerging markets: between 1% and 2% share in Germany and India.
  • Established markets: greater than 2% share in the U.K., the U.S., and South Korea.

When we look at the entire global footwear market, Crocs’ ability to take market share (as measured by revenue as a % of overall industry sales) in a highly fragmented market shines. Crocs has grown its share of the global footwear industry from 0.3% in 2018 to 1.0% in 2024. See Figure 2.

Figure 2: Crocs’ Global Market Share: 2018–2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Building A Larger Footprint

Crocs still generates most of its sales via its wholesale channel (selling to a retailer that sells to end users), though just barely. In 2024, wholesale sales represented 50.3% of revenue, down from 52% and 54.9% in 2023 and 2022, respectively.

Over that same time, the company has increased sales through its direct-to-consumer (DTC) channel, which includes both e-commerce and company owned retail stores. In 2022, DTC sales made up 45.1% of total revenue. In 2024, DTC made up 49.7% of total revenue.

Growing sales through its own retail channels allows the company to better control marketing, messaging, brand image, and even sell discontinued or discounted products through its outlet retail locations. To better serve customers, Crocs has grown its global store count (including both Crocs and HEYDUDE brand stores) from 367 in 2019 to 442 in 2024. See Figure 3.

In the first quarter of 2025, the DTC revenues for Crocs and HEYDUDE brands increased 1.1% and 8.3% YoY, respectively.

Figure 3: Crocs’ Global Store Count: 2019 – 2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Maintaining Pricing Power

In 2024, the Crocs brand accounted for 80% of revenue, while the HEYDUDE brand accounted for 20% of revenue.

Often, a business can grow market share by lowering prices to maximize quantity of sales. Crocs takes a different approach and has routinely taken market share by growing unit sales while its average selling price (ASP) rises.

On a yearly basis, Crocs brand unit sales almost doubled from 67 million in 2019 to 127 million in 2024, while its ASP increased from $18 to $26 over the same time. See Figure 4.

Both increasing unit sales and ASP drive Crocs brand’s revenue up from $1.2 billion in 2019 to $3.3 billion in 2024.

Figure 4: Crocs Brand Unit Sales and Average Selling Price: 2019 – 2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

The Lagging Elephant in the Room

While the Crocs brand has achieved excellent sales growth, the same cannot be said for HEYDUDE, the popular footwear brand Crocs acquired in 2022.

In 2024, both unit sales and revenue of the HEYDUDE brand declined year-over-year (YoY). However, the brand remains nearly a billion-dollar segment (based on revenues), and signs of a turnaround are present.

As with the Crocs brand above, management has not resorted to price cuts simply to drive unit sales. Instead, HEYDUDE’s ASP grew YoY in 2024 and increased for the seventh consecutive quarter in 1Q25. It’s clear that management values protecting margins, rather than chasing unit sales (more details later in report).

Despite the sales decline in 2024, HEYDUDE remains popular across e-commerce platforms. For instance, the brand was ranked as a top 3 brand on TikTok Shop in December 2024 and had its first-ever TikTok Shop Super Brand Day in February 2025, where it ranked as the no. 1 footwear brand across the platform.

The number of TikTok shoppers in the U.S. grew an estimated 34% YoY in 2024, and the number of TikTok shoppers is estimated to grow 6% compounded annually through 2027. By leveraging a growing presence across social media, along with bringing in new “brand ambassadors”, Crocs is positioning HEYDUDE for future growth opportunities.

Improving Fundamentals

Despite the HEYDUDE challenge, Crocs is growing top- and bottom-line.

The company has grown revenue by 14% and net operating profit after-tax (NOPAT) by 15% compounded annually since 2006 (earliest data available).

More recently, the company improved its NOPAT margin from 4% in 2014 to 21% in the TTM, while invested capital turns fell from 1.3 to 1.0 over the same time. Rising NOPAT margins are enough to offset falling invested capital turns, though, and drive return on invested capital (ROIC) from 5% in 2014 to 22% in the TTM.

Additionally, the company’s Core Earnings grew 41% compounded annually from $23 million in 2014 to $777 million in the TTM.

Figure 5: Crocs’ Revenue and NOPAT Since 2014

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Best-In-Class Profitability

Crocs not only has more global market share than peers such as Birkenstock (BIRK) and Steve Madden (SHOO), 0.5% and 0.6% market share in 2024, respectively, but it is also one of the most profitable companies in the casual footwear industry.

Crocs has the highest NOPAT margin and second highest ROIC among publicly traded peers, which include Deckers Outdoor (DECK), Nike (NKE), Birkenstock (BIRK), Steve Madden (SHOO), VF Corporation (VFC), and more. See Figure 6.

Figure 6: Crocs’ Profitability Vs. Peers: Trailing 12 Months (TTM)

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Repurchase Yield Could Reach 4% Or More

Crocs has not paid and does not plan to pay dividends on its common stock.

However, the company does return capital to shareholders through share repurchases.

Crocs has repurchased $2.1 billion (37% of market cap) shares since 2019. In February 2025, Crocs’ Board of Directors approved a $1 billion increase to the company’s share repurchase authorization. As of March 31, 2025, the company has $1.3 billion remaining under this authorization.

If the company repurchases shares at the same rate as 2024, when it repurchased $552 million in shares, it would repurchase an additional $492 million worth of shares in 2025 (in addition to the $61 million repurchased in 1Q25). $492 million in share repurchases represents 8.6% of the company’s current market cap.

If instead the company repurchases shares on par with its 1Q25 rate, it would repurchase $243 million worth of shares over the next 12 months, which represents 4.2% of the company’s current market cap.

Crocs paused share repurchases while it completed its acquisition of HEYDUDE, but outside of that year, management has consistently repurchased shares as the company’s stock has gotten cheaper. Per Figure 7, share repurchases have risen as CROX’s price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio has fallen.

Figure 7: Crocs’ Share Repurchases and PEBV Ratio: 2019 – 2024

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Cash Flow Picking Up Post Acquisition

Since 2019, Crocs has generated a cumulative $3.0 billion in free cash flow (FCF) excluding acquisitions, which equals 39% of its enterprise value.

When we include the acquisition of HEYDUDE in 2022, Crocs’ cumulative FCF is $309 million over the same time.

Perhaps most impressively, Crocs has rapidly improved FCF in the years since the acquisition. In 2023 and 2024, Crocs generated $1.8 billion in FCF. Crocs generated an additional $39 million in FCF in 1Q25.

Aggressively Paying Down Debt

Not only has Crocs used its strong free cash flow to repurchase shares, it is also paying down debt taken on for the acquisition of HEYDUDE.

Per Figure 8, Crocs’ total debt has declined from $2.8 billion in 2022 to $2.0 billion in 1Q25. We like companies that choose to return capital to shareholders and pay down debt, instead of spending it on exorbitant executive bonuses or more acquisitions.

Figure 8: Crocs’ Total Debt: 2022 – 1Q25

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Tariffs Could Potentially Hurt

Crocs management withdrew its full year 2025 guidance due to the uncertain impact of tariffs. However, management provided investors with an expense framework when they reported 1Q25 earnings. Specifically, management outlined two potential scenarios:

  1. “If we assume 10% incremental tariffs on all sourcing destinations into the US, this would translate to a cost of approximately $45 million on an annualized cash basis.”
  2. “If the incremental 145% tariff on China remains in place along with the 10% on all other sourcing destinations, this would imply a cost of approximately $130 million on an annualized cash basis based on our current sourcing mix.”

To offset tariff costs, management noted in the 1Q25 earnings call that they have already identified $50 million in potential savings throughout 2025.

Additionally, the company is focused on maintaining margins, rather than chasing sales volume, no matter what happens with tariffs.

From the 1Q25 earnings call, CEO Andrew Rees noted:

“I would rather increase prices, protect margin and have adequate room in the P&L to invest in marketing and consumer communication to drive consumers to our brand than take margin reductions and have no latitude to communicate effectively with consumers.”

Protecting margins at the potential expense of sales supports a strong brand image and reputation.

Though tariffs could add costs to Crocs’ business, the potential impact is already more than priced into the company’s stock price, as we’ll show below.

Current Price Implies Profits Will Permanently Fall By 20%

At its current price, Crocs’ price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio is 0.8. This ratio means the market expects the company’s NOPAT to permanently decline 20% from TTM levels. This expectation seems overly pessimistic, considering Crocs has grown NOPAT by 46% compounded annually over the last five years and 34% compounded annually over the last decade.

Below, we use our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to analyze expectations for different stock price scenarios for CROX.

In the first scenario, we quantify the expectations baked into the current price. If we assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 15% (well below TTM NOPAT margin of 21%) through 2034, and
  • revenue grows at 2.5% compounded annually through 2034 (equal to one-third of the forecasted casual footwear market CAGR of 7.4%) then

the stock is worth $102/share today – equal to the current stock price. In this scenario, Crocs’ NOPAT would fall 1% compounded annually from 2025 to 2034.

For reference, if we add $130 million (equal to the higher tariff costs provided by management) to Crocs’ operating expenses, the company’s TTM NOPAT margin would fall to 18.7% from 21.4% over the TTM.

In other words, Crocs’ current share price implies the company’s margins will fall even further than management expects and not recover for the next decade.

Shares Could Go 20%+ Higher Even If Tariffs Cut Margins

If we instead assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 18.7% (equal to implied margin in the worst tariff scenario outlined by management) through 2034,
  • revenue grows at consensus estimates in 2025 (1%), 2026 (3%), and 2027 (1%), and
  • revenue grows 2.5% each year thereafter through 2034 (equal to one-third of the forecasted casual footwear market CAGR of 7.4%), then

the stock would be worth $125/share today – a 23%+ upside to the current price. In this scenario, Crocs’ NOPAT would grow just 1% compounded annually through 2034. Should the company’s NOPAT grow more in line with historical levels, the stock has even more upside.

Figure 9 compares Crocs’ implied future NOPAT in these scenarios to its historical NOPAT.

Figure 9: Crocs’ Historical and Implied NOPAT: DCF Valuation Scenarios

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Sustainable Competitive Advantages Will Drive Shareholder Value Creation

Here’s a summary of why we think the moat around Crocs’ business will enable it to continue to generate higher NOPAT than the current market valuation implies:

  • consistently taking market share in a growing market,
  • well-positioned in the casual shoes market around the world, and
  • expanding point of sale and store footprint across the globe

What Noise Traders Miss With Crocs

These days, fewer investors focus on finding quality capital allocators with shareholder-aligned corporate governance. Due to the proliferation of noise traders, the focus is on short-term technical trading trends while more reliable fundamental research is overlooked. Here’s a quick summary of what noise traders are missing:

  • growing demand for comfortable and casual footwear,
  • superior profitability compared to peers – both margins and ROIC, and
  • valuation implies profits will permanently decline by 20%.

Tariff Certainty Could Send Shares Higher

The on-again, off-again tariffs have wreaked havoc on management teams’ ability to reliably forecast sales and profits for 2025 and beyond. Should the market receive any certainty on tariffs, especially if they’re lower than the worst-feared 145%, it could send many retail stocks, including CROX, higher.

Exec Comp Could Be Improved

Crocs’ executives receive both annual incentives and long-term equity awards. The annual incentives are tied to adjusted earnings before interest and tax (EBIT), adjusted free cash flow, brand strategic initiative performance, and corporate responsibility and sustainability progress.

Long-term equity awards are tied to adjusted 3-year cumulative revenue and adjusted EBITDA operating margin.

We would prefer the company tie executive compensation to ROIC. Doing so ensures that executives’ interests are more aligned with shareholders’ interests, as there is a strong correlation between improving ROIC and increasing shareholder value. Improving ROIC requires attention to all areas of the business, so including it in an executive compensation plan would incentivize executives to improve the entire business, not just one sales goal or earnings number.

Despite using revenue and EBIT goals in its executive pay, Crocs’ management has grown economic earnings, the true cash flows of the business, 52% compounded annually over the last five years, from $53 million in 2019 to $472 million in the TTM ended 1Q25.

Insider Trading And Short Interest Trends

Over the past 12 months, insiders have purchased 326,529 shares and sold 151,063 shares for a net effect of 175,466 shares bought. These sales represent <1% of shares outstanding.

There are currently 6.0 million shares sold short, which equates to 11% of shares outstanding and just under three days to cover.

Attractive Funds That Hold CROX

The following funds receive an Attractive-or-better rating and allocated significantly to CROX:

  1. Catalyst/Lyons Tactical Allocation Fund (CLTIX) – 3.4% allocation and Attractive rating.
  2. Acquirers Fund (ZIG) – 3.3% allocation and Very Attractive rating.
  3. Wall Street EWM Evercore Equity Fund (EWMCX) – 2.6% allocation and Attractive rating.
  4. Invesco S&P MidCap 400 GARP ETF (GRPM) – 3.3% allocation and Very Attractive rating.
  5. Alpha Architect U.S. Quantitative Value ETF (QVAL) – 2.0% allocation and Very Attractive rating.

data.title: VNM And Nike: Vietnam Trade Deal Good, But Not Great, News
data.description:
President Trump announced a trade deal with Vietnam on 2nd July, as reported here by The New York Times, just a week before the tariff deadline. The deal removes the threat of 45% tariffs but still imposes tariffs of 20% on imports from Vietnam.

Importantly, this now provides certainty to companies and investors of the outlook for both U.S.-based companies, and their manufacturing partners in Vietnam.

I see this as very positive news for U.S. companies with significant Vietnam exposure, like NIKE, Inc. (NYSE:NKE), but also for Vietnamese companies, as represented buy the VanEck Vietnam ETF (BATS:VNM).

However, when looking into the details, it seems that the direct impact of the deal is relatively limited, and in the case of Nike, already priced in.

Nike

As I wrote in an article back in May, Nike is particularly exposed to the potential tariffs.

According to Nike, the major manufacturing locations for Nike are Vietnam with around 50% of its shoes are made by 460,000 workers, Indonesia, with about 280,000 workers being about half the size of Vietnam, and China.

As Chinese labor has become less competitive vis-à-vis Vietnam and Indonesia, Nike has shifted work away, and now 100,000 employees in China mainly service the domestic market.

This makes the China's 145% tariff less relevant, however, exports from Vietnam and Indonesia face a 46% and 32% tariff respectively.

With the trade deal in place, an ongoing 20% tariff will be charged to imports from Vietnam, as long as the actual manufacturing takes place there.

As discussed in my previous article, research published by The Oregonian, in Nike's back yard, estimated that the potential for tariff impact could be up to 95% of Nike's profits.

Prior to the trade deal announcement, Nike had reported earnings, along with an estimated $1bn tariff related cost increase. A large part of this would be related to Vietnam. About half of Nike's products are manufactured in Vietnam, and about 40% are sold in North America.

Mitigation plans included price hikes for customers, which would be bound to impact fragile demand.

The market loved the clarity of outlook, and the stock reacted strongly post earnings. The release also coincided with the announcement of a China trade deal (however this is not really relevant to Nike due the production mix relocation to Vietnam and Indonesia).

The 20% tariff now implies that this $1bn cost would reduce to about $685m, which provides further good news for the stock outlook. To put this in context, TTM earnings were $3.2bn, so the Vietnam tariff news is worth about 10% uptick in earnings. That's the major tariff impact dealt with the next cab on the rank is Indonesia, about 25% of production, current tariff 32%. If we assume that there's an eventual deal with a 20% tariff, the potential reduction to the estimated $1bn earnings impact, now $685m post Vietnam, is only around $50m.

The stock price improved by a further 4.4% on 2nd.

I think that when the market does the math on the Vietnam exposure for Nike, the upside is now priced in.

However, when compared to key competitor adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY), we see a round trip now in terms of price, which can be seen to show severe tariff-related deviation from the time of the announcements.

The picture is similar on an absolute value basis. adidas and Nike price earnings are converging. Both look expensive on a TTM basis.

In terms of future valuation, the forward price earnings of Nike is hitting recent peaks. Either earnings estimates are wrong, or the market is likely to mean revert.

And analysts have upgraded Nike since earnings.

In general, analyst pricing and market pricing have been fairly close, but the stock price has regularly hit a ceiling at the analyst price target.

In my view, rotation and market exuberance is likely to push Nike a bit further, but I see limited long term upside from here.

I rate Nike a hold at this point, to be revisited when post tariff earnings and the new strategic direction flow through.

Let's have a look at the other side of the deal.

Vietnam - VNQ

The other side of the coin is what this deal means for Vietnam, and for investors in that market.

For readers not familiar with Vietnam as a market, I'd suggest putting history aside. I was going to say forget history, but that is never a wise move.

Modern day Vietnam has moved to take over from China as a location for outsourced manufacturing.

Foreign direct investment inflows to Vietnam have grown at a CAGR of 10% since 2006. This annual capital inflow was over 7% of GDP in 2022, the last year I can get records for.

Nike is the poster child to explain this investment inflow, having shifted production from mainly China, to mainly Vietnam over recent years, as Vietnamese labor has been more cost effective.

GDP growth has been phenomenal. Q1 2025 GDP growth was just under 7%.

And is expected to grow by 5%+ annually through 2030.

Vietnam is a great option for juicing a portfolio with growth in good old fashioned manufacturing stocks. So how to get in on the action?

While there is a local stock exchange in Hanoi and another in Ho Chi Minh (Saigon) foreign investors would probably be better advised to invest via an ETF or managed fund.

The top ETF for Vietnam is from VanEck, its VNM ETF.

This is a mid-sized ETF with just over $425m AUM, and a 0.68% expense ratio.

Holdings are heavily weighted to real estate and financials, which account for nearly 65% of the holdings. Manufacturing/industrial stocks are under 10%.

So the ETF is more exposed to GDP growth than to manufacturing directly.

Looking to the GDP drivers, the picture is a bit more weighted to manufacturing. GDP contribution from manufacturing has been stable at just under 40%.

The overall conclusion that I draw is that the trade deal is clearly important for the Vietnam economy as a while, with close to 40% of the GDP in manufacturing. Good news for the Vietnamese economy is systemically good for VNM, but the direct exposure of the ETF is somewhat limited.

The market reacted favorably following the trade news, but not over-exuberantly. An initial 2.5% surge on 2nd was erased on the day.

While over the last five years the performance of VNM has been flat, year to date, VNM has significantly outperformed, with a 20% YTD increase, even through the tariff dramas.

On the valuation side, we see a P/E multiple of just under 16x. So VNM is cheaper than SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), but with a sector weighting skewed to real estate and financials. 16x seems like a full valuation, even with higher expected earnings growth driven by a growing economy.

My conclusion is that there is no real impetus to buy VNM on the trade deal news. I would rate VNM a Hold.

Summary

Overall, while the Vietnam trade deal makes good headlines, and is of course a positive for Vietnam stocks and companies like Nike that have heavy Vietnam exposure, the overall impact seems to have been priced in. I wouldn't see this deal as a catalyst to Buy either Nike or VNM.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bentley Systems: Expanding E365 In Enterprise Customers; Initiate With 'Hold'
data.description:
Bentley Systems, Incorporated (NASDAQ:BSY) is an infrastructure lifecycle software company, that went public in September 2020. I favor Bentley’s double-digit growth in both its revenue and annualized recurring revenue (ARR), driven by its innovative platform and E365 solution. However, considering its current stock valuation, I am initiating with a ‘Hold’ rating with a fair value of $51 per share.

Bentley has prioritized E365 enterprise accounts in its growth initiatives. As depicted in the slide below, the E365 as a percentage of total ARR increased from 25% in FY20 to 45% in FY24. Through the E365 subscription, Bentley offers an all‑inclusive consumption‑based subscription plan for larger organizations. I believe E365 provides a comprehensive software solution for enterprise customers to manage their assets and infrastructure lifecycles. A growing number of E365 accounts demonstrates Bentley’s sales capabilities in upselling its solutions.

Bentley has done a great job of developing its direct sales team, with 93% of the total revenue generated by internal sales. I favor the company’s direct distribution model, which could reduce reliance on channel partners. The direct distribution model could enable the company to develop strong relationships with large enterprise clients and gain direct business insights, which are mission-critical for a lifecycle management software company, in my view.

As detailed in the table below, Bentley has experienced consistent double-digit constant revenue growth over the past four years, with strong growth in software subscriptions. The strong growth was driven by their account expansion, improved dollar-based renewal rate as well as the business transition from a perpetual license model to SaaS subscriptions, in my view.

As shown in the table above, the ARR has grown significantly over the past few years. One of the major growth drivers is the shift in account scale mix, with more revenue generated from large accounts. As depicted in the pie charts below, the revenue from large accounts (>$1mn per year) increased significantly from 2019 to 2024. During its Q1 FY25 earnings call, the management highlighted its ability to serve large accounts more deeply and efficiently, which could potentially accelerate its ARR growth and ongoing penetrations into enterprise customers.

In April 2025, Bentley announced its partnership with Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) to leverage Google Maps Platform for the detection and analysis of roadway conditions. In addition, Google’s Vertex AI will be integrated into Bentley's asset analytics platform for the road network. I view the partnership as a smart strategic move for Bentley, as its platform could combine Google Street View imagery, Vertex AI, and public road constructions. I anticipate Bentley will continue to integrate more AI-related features into its infrastructure lifecycle platform, which could potentially increase ARR and add further value for its enterprise customers.

Bentley released its Q1 FY25 result on May 7, posting 10.9% constant revenue growth and 12% constant ARR growth, as detailed in the table below:

My key takeaway from the quarter was the management’s confidence in growing ARR and the E365 program. During the call, the management mentioned that E365 renewal activities were robust. In addition, the company added 300bps of ARR growth from new logos during the quarter, primarily in the SMB market. It is impressive to see the company growing its ARR in both large enterprises and SMB markets.

Bentley is guiding for 10.5%-12.5% growth in both subscription revenue and ARR for FY25, as detailed in the slide below. I think the strong financial outlook is supported by their strong ARR momentum and E365 renewal activities.

For the normalized growth, I anticipate the company will grow its revenue by 9.5% organically, driven by 6% growth from dollar-based renewals and 3.5% growth from new logos in both large enterprises and SMBs. Data Insights Market predicts the Infrastructure software market will grow at a CAGR of 9.5% from 2025 to 2033. I anticipate Bentley will grow in line with the market growth, on an organic basis. In addition, I anticipate the company will allocate 6% of revenue towards M&A, contributing to an additional 2% growth to the topline, aligning with its historical trend.

I forecast the company will expand its margin by 40bps per year, driven by 10bps from pricing increase, 20bps from operating leverage of SG&A expenses and 10bps from cost reduction due to internal implementation of AI. During the call, the management noted that the company has been implementing AI technology, which could save 10%-20% of its developers’ time, which could speed up the new product development cycle and improve margins.

Bentley has spent more than 5% of revenue on stock-based compensations (SBC) for the past three years. This resulted in a 1% year-over-year increase in the total number of basic shares outstanding in FY23 and another 0.8% increase in FY24. As such, I estimate its total number of basic shares will increase by 0.5% annually from FY25 onwards, assuming the SBC will continue to dilute its shares outstanding.

With these assumptions, the DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate FCFF as follows:

The WACC is estimated to be 9.2% assuming a risk-free rate of 4.2%; beta of 0.9; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $17bn; debt $1.38bn; tax rate of 21%. I set the terminal growth to be 5%, aligning with the overall software market growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $51 per share, as per my estimate.

Downside Risks

The infrastructure lifecycle software company is controlled by several large players, such as Autodesk, Inc. (ADSK) in the US and Hexagon AB (publ) (OTCPK:HXGBF) and Dassault Systèmes SE (OTCPK:DASTY) in Europe. All these companies are competing in public works/utilities, resources and industrial markets. As the lifecycle software is project-based, it is relatively easy for customers to switch its software platform from one vendor to another. To sustain ARR growth, Bentley needs to continually invest in R&D, especially in the AI and cloud computing areas. Bentley’s shareholders should monitor its competitors’ activities to ensure Bentley’s competitive advantage remains strong.

According to its 10-K, 27% of total ARR is in the resource market, including mining, oil and gas industries. Due to energy transition and renewable trends, the traditional upstream customers might face growth challenges and reduce their IT spending on lifecycle management software, which could potentially adversely affect Bentley’s growth.

Lastly, Bentley had a net debt leverage of 2.4x including convertible notes due 2026/2027, as illustrated in the slide below. As a software company, I prefer the company to maintain a zero-debt balance sheet. On the positive side, the company has laid out a financial target to reduce its debt leverage to 0.7x over time.

Conclusion

I view Bentley Systems as a high-quality software company in the infrastructure lifecycle management industry. However, due to the current valuation, I am waiting for a better entry point. I am initiating with a ‘Hold’ rating with a fair value of $51 per share.

data.title: SPTI: Oil The Key Factor In The Inflation Fight
data.description:
The SPDR® Portfolio Intermediate Term Treasury ETF (NYSEARCA:SPTI) has a decent duration of around five years with a modest expense ratio of 0.03%. This is a lot more efficient in terms of expense ratios compared to the iShares comparable duration ETFs also exposed to Treasuries, which have a 0.15% expense ratio. In terms of the appeal of duration at the moment, the key matter of the oil prices as they related to the tensions of the Middle East has been settled for now, and we think this will have a critical effect on keeping inflation sufficiently in check to prevent major duration risks to duration bets like the SPTI.

SPTI Breakdown

The duration of the SPTI is just below 5 years, and appears to be a good option in terms of costs compared to the iShares ETFs of similar duration. This is a Treasury ETF, so we effectively ignore credit risk considerations, even though the U.S. Government is not AAA rated anymore. There is still a spread between AA rated corporate debt and Treasuries, which remain the benchmark, and might as well continue to be considered risk-free, to the extent that they've always been (not been).

CPI is still moderate. There isn't much MoM movement despite tariff effects ranging from the base 10% to the higher tariffs that exist on specific products, and perhaps the reciprocal tariffs that may come into effect at the end of the month absent trade deals with major trading nations like Japan.

A big part of the limited growth in CPI has been that oil prices (CO1:COM) (CL1:COM) have been significantly reduced compared to last year's comps. The significance of a $10 decline in oil prices equates to around a 0.5% negative vector on CPI. Oil is down around $15 per barrel as of today. The Iran-Israel exchange of fire put pressure on oil supply as speculators considered a risk to the passage of oil through the Strait of Hormuz, which concerns more countries than just Iran, and accounts for a very significant portion of global oil supply.

If oil prices remained at those elevated levels, CPI could have been bumped up almost by a whole percentage point. 2.4% is almost within the 2% target level, but 3.4% would have been a considerable level of inflation that the Fed would have to treat with the utmost gravity. This would preclude any possibility of rate cuts whatsoever, as the wage-price and other flywheel effects would have remained at large and threatened persistently high inflation.

Bottom Line

Even at 2.4% the economy is still not out of the woods. But moderate levels, that should look better on a more sustained basis, where American expectations about inflation will not be affected by higher gas prices at the pump, with jobs data still relatively under control in terms of continued openings for jobs and limited unemployment growth, mean that Fed cuts are possible. Openings for jobs unexpectedly rose and firings are still pretty limited, which means corporates are not yet in trouble.

The market clearly agrees with this view, given the recent translations downward of the yield curve. Continued yield curve movements downward are clearly what one would hope for when taking positions in higher duration fixed income instruments. As such, there would be risks to SPTI in that issues in the Middle East could flare up once again and threaten oil movements. But for it to become a problem for the American consumer to the point where it changes the calculation for the Fed would require a more sustained problem, though the Fed may already be more conservative with using its tools to accommodate the economy, given that there remain heightened risks of tensions as the Israel-Hamas war is still ongoing. The Israel-Iran situation, though, seems to be at a geopolitical equilibrium, also based on the position of foreign powers, allies and backers, and given that the Iranian regime was put in serious trouble and was forced to relent.

But for the expectations factor of inflation, the spot price of oil and the price of oil at the pump are what matters. So long as CPI can continue at these more moderate levels, the consciousness of the possibility of a sub 2% inflation rate from the consumer remains a realistic assumption and gives space to rate cutting hopes.

data.title: Strategic Education: Debt-Free, Scalable, And Ready For Growth
data.description:
After a five-day road trip with my wife in the countryside, I'm back and ready to analyze yet another promising EdTech for you.

And you know what's funny? The last time we checked out an EdTech, it turned out pretty well. QuantaSing Group Limited (QSG) has already jumped over 100%, all thanks to its smart focus on the ‘Silver Economy’. Sure, the acquisition of Letsvan helped a ton—it marked their official move into consumer goods and was a big reason for the rally.

But here's the thing: there are still a bunch of overlooked names out there trading at dirt-cheap levels. You just need to look beyond the usual Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL) and Pearson plc (PSO) and you might see some undercovered gems.

Today we’re diving into Strategic Education, Inc. (NASDAQ:STRA)—a bit of a sleeper in the market.

Despite having a market cap north of $2 billion, it’s flying under the radar. No Seeking Alpha analyst has touched it in nearly two years. Let's roll up our sleeves and do it then.

Starting where it all began, Strategic Education's foundation dates back to the founding of Strayer Business College in Baltimore, Maryland back in 1892. The focus was to meet the growing demand for accounting professionals following the productivity boom at the turn of the century.

In 2018, another company crossed paths with Strayer. I’m talking about Capella Education Company.

Unlike Strayer, Capella was all-in on online learning, offering everything from undergraduate to master’s and even doctoral programs. That merger is what gave rise to the Strategic Education we know today.

The deal was worth $1.9 billion and was completed with stock only. Strategic Education was formed with Strayer shareholders owning 52% and Capella shareholders owning 48%, keeping the universities as separate institutions.

This merger caused revenues to grow more than 57% year over year in FY 2019, reaching $1 billion the following year.

But was it just the merger with Capella that sparked that big revenue jump? Definitely not. If I left out their international expansion, I’d be leaving out a huge part of the story.

In mid-FY 2020, Strategic Education completed the acquisition of Laureate Education’s (operator of Torrens University, Think Education, and Media Design School) operations in Australia and New Zealand, expanding well beyond the United States.

To get these assets in its pocket, the EdTech company shelled out just over $600 million, partly financed by issuing new shares and long-term debt that has already been repaid to date.

Alright, alright. We’ve already covered the brands, but what about the products? That’s the cherry on the cake. Strategic’s offerings make it clear that the company is laser-focused on corporate education.

That’s what really sets it apart: building B2B partnerships to help companies upskill their workforce.

We can’t help but mention Sophia Learning, an online platform that offers college-credit courses designed to be affordable and self-paced with a focus on career progression.

But perhaps the real icing on the cake is WorkForce Edge.

In short, it is an educational benefits management platform aimed at companies that offer financial support to employees in courses and training. However, strategically speaking, it is much more than that.

  • It's basically a steady, reliable way to bring in B2B revenue (corporate students generally have higher retention and lower default rates). Companies want to boost employee skills through tuition assistance, and Strategic makes that easy by centralizing everything on one platform. It also helps employers track progress and manage reimbursements without the usual hassle;
  • This is a product with incredible scalability. Think about it: Strategic Education currently has 78 corporate partners (they added two new partners in Q1 FY 2025), with those companies totaling 3.9 million employees. WorkForce Edge has currently brought in 2,300 students at both Strayer and Capella. This is about 0.05% of the employees of the partner companies! Imagine if this continues to expand;
  • The direct connection to large employers makes WorkForce Edge a perfect product for a company that focuses so much on the job market. With this in mind, they can expect more enrollments in other employability-focused courses.

Now that you and Strategic have been properly introduced, let’s tackle the big question: is this EdTech in good financial shape?

Does Strategic Education Pass the Test?

Just by looking at it, we can notice some interesting things:

  1. Strategic Education has been a debt-free company since FY 2024. During the period from FY 2020 to FY 2023 the company maintained some debt due to the acquisition of Laureate's businesses in Australia and New Zealand;
  2. The company is also self-financing. If we look at the last decade, Strategic Education has generated positive FCF every year. This has led to them generating a cash surplus every year until FY 2020. This is the good old-fashioned way of an asset-light business with low Capex needs. After this year, they have been running a cash deficit that has been decreasing as they repay their long-term debt, and should start accumulating cash again in FY 2025. Furthermore, they have $182.6 million in cash and equivalents, which for a debt-free company, is a lot of liquidity;
  3. Recent trends (FY 2024) are pointing us towards sustained post M&A growth.

But we’re looking at more recent trends here, right?

In FY 2024, Strategic saw revenues grow nearly 7.7% year-over-year to $1.23 billion, driven primarily by its EdTech services (remember, this includes partnerships with companies, WorkForce Edge and Sophia Learning), while US Higher Education remained flat along with Australia and New Zealand.

It’s clear that while US Higher Education with Capella and Strayer remains the cash cow, the star is EdTech.

In the same period, they reported EBIT of $157.3 million (up 40% year-over-year) and this was largely impacted by increased revenue inflow from the EdTech segment with segment margins reaching 40%. This boosted EBIT margin by nearly 300 bp year-over-year to 12.9%.

But that's not all, normalizing the diluted EPS for the entire FY 2024 we find $4.22. This is the highest EPS in the last decade.

Looking ahead to Q1 FY 2025, does this look like it will continue? Yes, it does.

They reported revenue of $303.6 million (maintaining mid-single-digit growth year-over-year), yet they continued to see the benefits in margins from increased EdTech inflow. For example, adjusted operating income increased 16% year-over-year, and operating profit margin increased to 13.6%. But perhaps the biggest growth was in adjusted earnings per share, which jumped from $1.11 to $1.29.

As I explained, the key to understanding this is in the revenue streams. Just take a look at how that played out in Q1 FY 2025:

  • U.S. Higher Education (Strayer and Capella) revenues were $221 million in the quarter. This represented an increase of 0.8% year-over-year. This revenue stream had an adjusted operating profit margin of 13.6% and an adjusted operating income of $30 million (up 6.9% year-over-year);
  • The EdTech segment saw revenues of $34.3 million in Q1 FY 2025, a very strong 45.2% year-over-year growth. Operating margins in the segment were 40.3%, down 240 bp year-over-year and totaling an adjusted operating profit of $13.8 million (up 37.3% year-over-year);
  • Australia and New Zealand saw revenues of $48.3 million (up 1.9% year-over-year), with an adjusted operating margin of -4.3% and an operating loss of $2.1 million.

B2B: Strategic Education's Gold Mine

B2B partnerships are benefiting Strategic in every way.

In the United States, for example, the percentage of students affiliated with companies increased by approximately 7% year-over-year, with 31% of all enrolled students coming from corporate partners.

Sophia Learning, a major driver of the EdTech segment and a gateway to corporate partnerships (if the company offers some type of short-term, individualized training), increased its total average subscribers by 37% and its revenue by 36%.

As I mentioned, WorkForce Edge already has 78 corporate partners on board—and thanks to it, over 2,300 students have enrolled at Strayer or Capella. That’s a huge 50% year-over-year jump.

This is the heart of the whole thesis.

WorkForce Edge is super scalable and still just getting started. Right now, only about 0.05% of employees from those partner companies are actually enrolled.

In Q1 FY 2025, we got a taste of this with Best Buy Co., Inc. (BBY) joining a full partnership with Strayer University (all full- and part-time Best Buy employees earn a degree at no cost to the employee) and WorkForce Edge.

Dividends and Buybacks? Yes, They Pay!

With strong FCF, Strategic Education typically returns capital to shareholders in the form of dividends and buybacks.

Here are some key facts about this:

  • Strategic has $197 million remaining in repurchase authorization through the end of FY 2025. Last quarter, they used $32 million to repurchase approximately 390,000 shares of common stock, bringing their outstanding shares to 24 million. Assume they return this $197 million during FY 2025, repurchasing between 2 to 2.5 million shares depending on average repurchase price. This would bring their outstanding shares to 21.5 to 22 million, a return of over 8% by the end of the year;
  • An estimated dividend of $2.40. This is a yield of approximately 2.8% with a payout ratio of just over 47%. It is important to note that the company is not a good dividend payer. That is, they have paid consecutive dividends for only 7 years (less than the industry median of 11) and have not increased their dividend since 2019.

If we include dividends and buybacks, we're looking at roughly a 10% return from now until year-end—not too shabby.

But let’s take a look at the valuation and see if this hidden gem is actually trading at a discount compared to its peers.

Discounted Relative to Peers? Let's Find Out

According to SA Quant, Strategic Education scores a B- on Valuation. That’s a solid starting point. However, to locate a price target, we need to select the right companies to compare multiples.

That said, I will use a series of companies from the for-profit higher education sector. They are:

  • Adtalem Global Education Inc. (ATGE);
  • Grand Canyon Education, Inc. (LOPE);
  • Perdoceo Education Corporation (PRDO);
  • Laureate Education, Inc. (LAUR);
  • American Public Education, Inc. (APEI).

Starting with the P/E, we note that the average of these five companies gives us an average multiple of 16.3x. Analysts expect EPS of $5.51 to $5.72 (consensus at $5.63) for the full FY 2025. This would give us a price target of approximately $91.80 for this stock.

Moving on to another equity multiple, the P/S of the comparable companies is 2.6x. Analysts estimate FY 2025 revenue between $1.26 billion and $1.28 billion (consensus $1.27 billion). With roughly 22 million shares outstanding at the end of FY 2025, I expect an RPS of $57.72. That gives us a price target of $150.

Sounds pretty high, doesn’t it? Excluding outliers like Grand Canyon, we have a 2x multiple, which gives us a price target of $115.

Jumping to EV multiples, let’s take a look at EV/EBIT. First, let’s assume an EBIT margin of 10% to 14%. With revenues estimated at $1.27 billion, EBIT would be projected at $127 to $177 million. Other assumptions we could make are about net debt. I expect them to be sitting on at least $200 million in cash and equivalents at the end of FY 2025, with that being negative net debt. And of course, 22 million shares outstanding at the end of the year.

The average comparable multiple for EV/EBIT is 12.3x. Using the low end of the EBIT estimates, we would have a price target of $80—very close to where the stock is trading today. Now, if we consider the high-end, we would have a price target of around $108.

Considering all metrics, we have the following price target perspective for Strategic Education:

A Headwind to Consider: Australia and New Zealand

Revenues from Australia and New Zealand make up around 20% of the company’s total—but that chunk is under some pressure right now.

But why? Simple. Changes in the regulatory environment. Let me explain.

Anthony Albanese, Australia’s Prime Minister, assumed office with a commitment to implementing stricter controls on international student admissions—especially at Aussie universities, where a large share of students come from nearby China. Now, a coalition is pushing to cap international enrollments at 240,000 per year.

That’s not great news for Strategic Education, especially considering they entered the region just five years ago. A large part of their student base there tends to be international, so this policy shift could hit them where it hurts.

Strategic will focus more on Australian and New Zealand students, but we can safely expect enrollments to decline by low to mid-single digits for the rest of the year.

Bottom Line

Putting it all together, we’re looking at a potential upside of 26.5% just from capital gains if the stock hits my $105 price target. Now tack on another 10% or so from dividends and buybacks, and you’ve got a total return approaching 40% by the end of 2025.

With tailwinds from the EdTech segment (particularly from WorkForce Edge partnerships), we have a strong outlook for FY 2025. And perhaps best of all: a squeaky-clean balance sheet capable of both M&A and returning capital to shareholders.

That said, I’m starting my coverage of Strategic Education with a ‘Strong Buy’ rating.

data.title: NYLI Epoch Capital Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI World Index fell 1.7% over the first quarter of 2025. Equities sold off in the second half of Q1 as enthusiasm faded post Trump's inauguration, replaced instead by unease arising from tariff uncertainty, creeping doubts in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) secular growth story fueled by the release of DeepSeek, softer economic data, and depressed sentiment. Cyclical pockets expressed this hit, with discretionary industries underperforming many defensives. Caution was further indicated by the various downward revisions of growth forecasts by corporate and central banks alike, coupled with negative earnings revisions. The American stock market was the main laggard this quarter as the megacap tech names pulled back on reduced faith in the payoff of AI capex spending. U.S. markets were further plagued by tariff and policy uncertainty, which caused some rotation to favor ex-U.S. investments as well as revision upward of inflation expectations. Europe outperformed, particularly with the added boosts of German expansionary fiscal impulse, accommodative central banks, and a rally by European defense stocks in anticipation of increased military spending. However, European economic indicators remain sluggish, with muted growth forecasts and continued monetary easing attempting to combat it. Asian equity performance was more of a mixed bag over the quarter, with China and Hong Kong the standouts and Japan, Taiwan, and South Korea trailing behind. China and Hong Kong indices were particularly propelled by technology boons like DeepSeek's announced AI advancements and fiscal support for the sector. The region's economic data, however, reflected a slight claw back in retail sales and industrial output, but continues to show labor market weakness. The top performing sectors this quarter included energy (+10.1%), utilities (+7.2%), and financials (+6.1%).

Information technology (-11.9%), consumer discretionary (-10.3%), and communications services (-4.4%) all lagged this quarter due in part to the correction in the Magnificent 7** names.

**(Alphabet (Google)(GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta platforms (Facebook) (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA) & Tesla (TSLA))

Portfolio Highlights

  • The NYLI Epoch Capital Growth Fund (the “Fund”) underperformed its benchmark by ~1.0% in the first quarter. This consisted of a negative impact from factor exposures, that was partially offset by a positive contribution from stock-specific risk. Industry and style factors both detracted, while country and currency exposures contributed positively.
  • Over the course of the quarter, the trades we made, together with the impact of the varying performance across stocks and sectors, caused our weights in the health care and consumer staples sectors to rise by 2.5% and 0.7%, respectively. Meanwhile, our weight in the information technology sector fell by 2.0% and our weight in consumer discretionary fell by 1.5%. As of March 31st, our biggest overweight was in health care (+8.2%), followed by consumer discretionary (6.7%) and industrials (+4.5%). We were underweight in almost every other sector, led by financials (-6.0%) and information technology (-4.3%), followed by utilities (-2.7%), real estate (-2.2%), communication services (-2.1%), and materials (-1.7%). As we always note, none of the sector weightings reflect a top-down view, but instead result from the individual holdings identified by our investment process, together with the overall risk management that takes place during portfolio construction.
  • On the other hand, something that very much is intentional is the Fund's wide Return on Invested Capital - Weighted Average Cost of Capital (ROIC-WACC) spread compared to the benchmark. As of March 31st, the Fund's weighted-median aggregate spread was 18.4%, compared to 7.8% for the MSCI World Index.

What Helped:

  • Looking at country and currency exposures, the underweight to the United States helped performance amid the sell-off, in addition to overweights to Spain and China. The underweight to Germany was the top detracting country exposure as the administration kicked up fiscal support for the economy. The overweights to the Swedish krona, Mexican peso, and British pound contributed to active returns as well.
  • Positively contributing industry exposures included the overweight to Health Care Providers & Services and underweights to Technology Hardware and Software. The positive exposure to profitability contributed 0.2% in active return.
  • As for stock-specific risk, BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), Chemed (CHE), BayCurrent (OTCPK:BYCRF), and Yum Brands (YUM) all contributed positively to active performance during Q1, coupled with the portfolio's underweight positions in the Magnificent 7. BYD released a few positive announcements in February that boosted its share price, including strong unit sales growth and the advancement of its AI-powered smart driving system. The company also continues to enjoy the windfalls of competitive pricing and favorable valuation, making it a preferred source of Electric Vehicle (EV) exposure over peers.

What Hurt:

  • Industry exposures collectively detracted 1.5%. The underweights to Aerospace & Defense and Insurance, in addition to the overweight to Life Sciences Tools & Services, inflicted the largest negative impact on active return. The underweight to Banks also detracted. Within styles, negative exposures to momentum and value detracted, along with the positive market sensitivity exposure. Other all, The underweights to the Japanese yen and euro detracted from active returns.
  • The stocks that caused the largest detractions were Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK), eMemory, Arista Networks (ANET), and West Pharmaceutical (WST). Deckers shares have been under pressure year-to-date, largely reflecting fiscal fourth quarter guidance offered at the end of January that was more cautious than the market had anticipated. We believe the lower-than-expected guidance is largely a function of the timing of new product launches and suggests a lower growth rate than the company has had in recent years. An uncertain consumer environment and the prospect of tariffs may have also weighed on shares in the near-term. We continue to have faith in the company due to the Hoka brand's robust innovation slate to be launched through 2025, meaningful international expansion opportunities, and healthy profitability in the form of high ROIC.

Top Contributors and Detractors (%)

Outlook and Positioning:

  • Equity markets began the year on a positive note, but the trend changed dramatically from February 19th as the focus shifted to trade wars. This weakness was initially concentrated in U.S. markets but has now spread to all major equity markets globally.
  • Overall, we believe the trade war will proceed in three stages, beginning with tariff hikes. The second stage, which we've just entered, is about negotiating deals, with the stock market likely to bottom once real progress is signaled. On the other hand, lack of progress means the "America alone" scenario and suggests further downside. The third stage, likely to begin in Q3, emphasizes Trump's pro-growth agenda, which includes tax cuts, deregulation, shrinking the government, and pursing pro-tech policies. If this, admittedly optimistic, scenario is realized, equities could be significantly above today's level by year-end.
  • Turning to market fundamentals, consensus earnings forecasts do not yet adequately incorporate recent developments in the trade war. For example, the S&P 500 Index is still expected to grow Earnings Per Share (EPS) this year by 12% (marked down from 15% last month) and 14% (unchanged) in 2026. The corresponding EPS growth estimates are slightly lower for Europe (7% and 11%), Japan (10% and 9%) and China (8% and 12%), with these forecasts only revised marginally lower over the last month. It is highly likely that consensus 2025 EPS growth estimates will be slashed over coming months.
  • Regarding valuations, the 2025 12-month Forward Price Earnings ratio for the S&P 500 Index is currently 19x, just slightly above the historical mean of 17x. Similarly, the tech sector is now trading at 23x, down from 29x two months ago. Markets outside the U.S. look quite attractive, with Europe at 14x, Japan 13x, and China 12x. All are still trading at exceptionally large discounts to the U.S.
  • What does all this mean for the equity market outlook? Trade war induced uncertainty is likely to be with us for a few more months, if not quarters. However, Trump 2.0 tax cuts, a pro-tech bias and deregulation measures, as well as the "Fed put," amount to powerful reasons to expect the S&P 500 Index to appreciate by 10% before year-end.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. data.title: Reserve Bank Of Australia Preview: Next Week Provides Another Window To Cut Rates
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava & Francesco Pesole

Likely to be another high conviction rate cut

We expect the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) to cut the cash rate by 25bp to 3.6% at the upcoming policy meeting next week. Recent data flows on growth and inflation have been softer than expected.

The previous rate adjustment by the RBA was not only a reduction but a decisive one, with discussions centred around whether the cut should be 25bp or 50bp. In alignment with other central banks in the region, the RBA adopted a dovish stance on the growth trajectory due to tariff uncertainties and also revised its inflation forecasts downward.

Weaker growth and softer inflation reinforce our view

What adds conviction to our call next week is a much softer-than-expected CPI inflation reading for May. Headline CPI decreased from 2.4% in April 2025 to 2.1% in May 2025, nearing the lower end of the central bank's target range of 2-3%. High trimmed mean inflation reflecting high services inflation, which has been a more persistent issue, also declined to 2.4% year-over-year, indicating substantial progress in controlling inflation from a peak of 4.4% year-over-year one year ago.

First-quarter GDP growth was weak too, with quarter-on-quarter growth at just 0.2%, falling below expectations. Softer private consumption growth was partly seasonal and due to extreme weather events, but public spending has had the largest negative impact on growth since the September quarter of 2017, and net trade also dragged GDP growth lower.

However, employment data was strong, with wage growth rising to a solid 3.4% year-over-year in the first quarter. In May 2025, despite a small drop in overall job growth, full-time employment rose by about 39,000, which partly offset losses in part-time employment. Reduced underemployment and strong demand for hours worked also point to a healthy labour market.

Adding one more rate cut to the 2025 profile

Overall, the global backdrop on tariffs remains uncertain, and both headline and trimmed inflation have been firmly on a downtrend, remaining close to the midpoint of the 2-3% range over the next few quarters.

The labour data could start to turn as well. In the previous monetary policy statement, members observed that the rate of voluntary job turnover had declined and that, anecdotally, the focus of wage bargaining and employment disputes had tilted in favour of workers seeking greater job security. This might see wage growth slow more noticeably than currently forecast.

Given that the downside risks to both growth and inflation are more dominant at this stage, we are adding a 25bp rate cut to our 2025 profile, taking rates to a terminal level of 3.1% by the end of 2025.

AUD/USD may be close to a peak

Risks for AUD around the 9 July US tariff deadline are no greater than for other high-beta currencies, and certainly not more significant than for USD.

AUD has experienced the largest drop in short-term swap rates across G10 since the start of the year: 65bp in the two-year tenor. Although a host of external drivers has sometimes obscured the FX impact, the sensitivity of AUD to rate differentials has increased, as reflected in our G10 short-term valuation model. While this is primarily a function of Fed repricing, a quieter backdrop on tariffs – barring any major escalation in July – means central bank policy is regaining its place as the main driver of relative value in risk-exposed currencies.

AUD front-end swap rates have fallen the most in G10 this year

Markets are currently pricing in 17bp of easing for this month’s RBA meeting and 78bp by year-end. We believe a cut next week could trigger a broader dovish repricing along the AUD OIS curve, with a more visible impact on AUD. On this basis, we expect AUD to lag behind NZD and potentially other high-beta currencies where there is less room for front-end rates to fall, such as SEK.

AUD/USD came close to touching our near-term target of 0.660, with the move still almost entirely driven by US developments. With US inflation, in our view, set to rebound in the coming months and drive a hawkish repricing in USD rates, we expect a recovery in USD. Coupled with a more dovish RBA – though this remains a secondary driver – we expect AUD/USD to move back to 0.650 this summer.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
