data.title: Cameco: The King Of Nuclear Ready, Thanks To AI And Geopolitics
data.description:
Why Cameco Is the Big Bet on Nuclear

Cameco Corporation (NYSE:CCJ) is one of the best opportunities to invest in the nuclear revival, destined to become a pillar of the global energy transition and security of supply. The geopolitical crisis has highlighted the vulnerability of traditional energy supply chains, pushing governments in the US, Europe, and Asia to reevaluate the role of nuclear as a stable source to ensure energy autonomy. The real AI revolution is not only technological but also energetic. The explosion in electricity demand linked to the growth of artificial intelligence and data centers is creating unprecedented pressure on the need for new low-emission production capacity.

Its strengths are certainly:

  1. First-tier uranium reserves in safe jurisdictions such as Canada.
  2. Long-term contract portfolio that offers visibility on revenues even in scenarios of spot price volatility.
  3. 49% strategic participation in Westinghouse, thus positioning itself as a global leader capable of providing not only raw materials, but also technology and services for new nuclear projects. With solid growth guidance for the coming years, robust cash flows, and a balance sheet capable of financing expansion without significant dilution, Cameco offers investors privileged exposure to a multi-year nuclear investment cycle.

Overview

Cameco is a leading global player in the uranium market, with integrated operations across the entire nuclear fuel cycle. The company operates tier-one mines such as McArthur River/Key Lake and Cigar Lake in Canada, which are considered among the richest and lowest-cost in the world, and engages in refining, conversion, and fuel fabrication through its Fuel Services division.

Completing its vertical integration, Cameco holds a 49% interest in Westinghouse Electric Company, a global leader in nuclear reactor technology and services, as detailed in the company’s Q1 2025 presentation.

As of December 31, 2024, the company controls more than 485 million pounds of proven and probable reserves, providing a sustainable production base for decades. The long-term contract portfolio is expected to average approximately 28 million pounds per year through 2029, with particularly strong levels in the 2025-2027 period.

Cameco earns the majority of its revenues in US dollars, providing a partial natural hedge against Canadian dollar volatility. Its disciplined strategy of signing contracts only under favorable market conditions maximizes margins and reduces dependence on spot price fluctuations.

Finally, its participation in Global Laser Enrichment provides an additional competitive advantage, positioning Cameco in key segments of the fuel cycle with innovative technologies.

Growing Earnings and Long-Term Contracts: How Cameco is Beating Expectations

Cameco’s Q1 2025 results confirmed the company’s operational and financial strength, demonstrating the resilience of the business even in the context of volatile uranium spot prices. According to the official results release, the company recorded:

  • Revenues of CAD 789 million, up 24% compared to Q1 2024.
  • Adjusted Net Earnings of CAD 70 million, +52% y/y.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of CAD 353 million, +5% y/y.

Uranium

The Uranium segment generated revenues of CAD 619 million (+10% y/y) thanks to an average realized price of CAD 89.12/lb (+15% y/y). Adjusted EBITDA was CAD 286 million, slightly down (-6%) mainly due to the lower contribution of the Inkai JV due to the temporary stop in January. However, long-term contracts allowed Cameco to benefit from prices above the spot market, confirming the effectiveness of the disciplined contracting strategy, as we said before.

Fuel Services

The Fuel Services segment showed exceptional expansion with revenues up to CAD 135 million (+88% y/y) and adjusted EBITDA to CAD 75 million (+200% y/y), thanks to sales volumes up 60% and a 17% increase in the average realized price (see presentation on the website) This improvement reflects the recovery of downstream activities and the consolidation of demand from utilities.

Segment Westinghouse

The contribution of the 49% stake in Westinghouse recorded a net loss reduced to -62 million CAD compared to -123 million a year earlier, with adjusted EBITDA increasing to 92 million CAD (+19% y/y). Cameco expects Westinghouse adjusted EBITDA (Cameco share) in the range of 355-405 million USD for 2025, confirming the progressive integration and the ability to generate significant cash flows from this strategic participation.

Guidance & Outlook

The production guidance for 2025 remains unchanged with 36 million pounds total expected from the McArthur River/Key Lake and Cigar Lake sites, while the Inkai JV revised the 2025 target to 8.3 million pounds (100% basis), of which 3.7 million from Cameco share, after the temporary suspension in January.

Valuation & Return Potential

According to Bloomberg Terminal, Cameco is currently trading at an estimated forward EV/EBITDA of 20.91x for FY2026.

I have developed two scenarios to estimate a fair value consistent with the company's growth prospects.

Baseline (conservative)

  • Estimated EBITDA FY2026: CAD2.071 billion (Bloomberg).
  • Target multiple: 20.91x EV/EBITDA (in line with current market valuations).
  • Enterprise Value (EV) = CAD2,071 million × 20.91 = CAD43,301 million.
  • Estimated net debt FY2026: -CAD 497 million.
  • Equity Value = CAD43,301 million + CAD0.497 million = CAD 43,798 million.
  • The fair value per share = CAD43,798 million / 436 million shares = CAD100.5 share, or $74.4 per share (exchange rate 1 USD = 1.35 CAD).

Potential upside vs. current price (around 71 USD): +5%.

Bull scenario (optimistic)

  • Estimated EBITDA FY2026: we can assume a +15% vs. baseline guidance, in the event of a rise in spot uranium prices above $90/lb and an acceleration of long-term contracts. Both elements are supported by the market trends highlighted by Cameco on its official website and in the Q1 2025 presentation, which show a structural supply deficit expected as early as 2026-2027.
  • These factors make an FY2026 EBITDA of CAD2.381 billion plausible, which reflects an improvement compared to the base guidance thanks to volumes contracted at higher prices and greater use of conversion and fuel services assets.
  • Target multiple: 23x EV/EBITDA, consistent with the historical positive cycles of the sector.
  • EV = CAD2,381 million × 23 = CAD54,763 million
  • Equity Value = CAD54,763 million + CAD 0.497 million = CAD55,260 million.
  • The fair value per share = CAD55,260 million / 436 million shares = CAD126.7 per share, or $93.9 per share.

Potential upside vs. current price: +32%.

AI, Data Centers and the Energy Revolution: The Tailwinds for Uranium

The current macroeconomic and geopolitical context offers Cameco extremely favorable terrain to support uranium demand and strengthen margins.

Energy security and the new geopolitics of energy

The Russia-Ukraine crisis has highlighted the vulnerability of gas and coal supply chains, accelerating the search for solutions independent of geopolitically unstable suppliers.

Structural demand from AI and data centers

According to Goldman Sachs projections, the growth of artificial intelligence will lead to a 160% increase in data center electricity consumption by 2030. This pressure on global electricity demand will make stable and carbon-free energy sources such as nuclear essential, reinforcing the need for long-term contracts for uranium

Global political push for nuclear

The US, the European Union, China, India, and several Middle Eastern countries have announced new programs to build large reactors or Small Modular Reactors (SMR) within the next decade. The EU has included nuclear in the green taxonomy, confirming the political commitment to consider it an integral part of the energy transition.

Rigid supply and long-term contracts

Cameco highlights in the Q1 2025 presentation that the market is set to enter a structural deficit between uranium supply and demand as early as 2026, due to the lack of new significant mining projects and the operational difficulties of producers in geopolitically unstable areas such as Kazakhstan.

Risks & Opportunities

Key Risks

  • High Valuations: The company is priced at a forward EV/EBITDA of 20.91x, above its historical average of 18-19x (Bloomberg Terminal). Any disappointment on uranium prices or delays in signing new contracts could lead to a contraction in multiples.
  • Geopolitics Inkai JV: Cameco holds a 40% stake in the Inkai joint venture in Kazakhstan, a country with geopolitical risks, as demonstrated by the temporary suspension of operations in January 2025.
  • Regulatory or Construction Delays: New reactor or SMR projects may be slowed down for regulatory, financial, or public sentiment reasons, postponing expected near-term uranium demand growth.
  • Spot Price Volatility: While long-term contracts offer protection, a prolonged decline in spot prices could impact market sentiment and cause utilities to delay new deals, as explained in the “Market Dynamics” section of the Cameco website.

Key Opportunities

  • Rising Uranium Prices: Spot prices could rise above $90/lb in the next 12–24 months, generating margin leverage.
  • New Long-Term Contracts: Cameco stated in its Q1 2025 presentation that it has a significant pipeline of new LT contract deals (Investor Presentation), which could be accretive to EBITDA and improve future revenue visibility.
  • Westinghouse as a Catalyst: Westinghouse’s 49% stake provides direct exposure to new demand for reactor technology, both large and SMR, with growth opportunities in emerging markets and in existing reactor upgrade programs.

Strong Buy Rating: Cameco As a Strategic Investment for the Future of Energy

Cameco is the company par excellence if you want exposure to the uranium sector. The company benefits from a long-term contract portfolio that protect revenues even in volatile phases and a solid financial position, as evidenced by the results of the first quarter of 2025 and the confirmed guidance. The stake in Westinghouse guarantees additional exposure to the positive cycle of construction and renewal of nuclear reactors, while the market dynamics signal a structural deficit in uranium supply already from 2026, as illustrated in the Investor Presentation and in the Market Overview section of the company website.

Although current valuations already discount some potential, according to my calculations in the bull scenario, a further increase in uranium spot prices and an acceleration of LT contracts is possible that would push the fair value per share up to $94, with a potential upside of more than 30% from current levels. In the context of growing global electricity demand, driven by AI and digitalization, and political support for nuclear in several areas of the world, Cameco is well positioned to benefit from favorable secular trends.

For these reasons, I think Cameco deserves a Strong Buy rating, with a positive medium-long term view, based on solid fundamentals, macro catalysts, and the ability to generate value through disciplined business management. Recommended maximum weight of 5% in a well-diversified portfolio.

data.title: Capitalize On Rising Demand: Why Micron Is Poised For Growth
data.description:
I gave Micron (NASDAQ: MU) a buy recommendation in my last article on this stock due to the company's potential opportunity in High Bandwidth Memory ("HBM"), which could be massive, the likelihood that the memory industry is in an upcycle, and the favorable forward PEG ratio indicating the stock has substantial potential upside. The company's third-quarter earnings report confirmed those theses.

As a result, the stock has increased by 82.41% since my recommendation on April 21, 2025, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 20.71% rise as of the July 2 market close. Looking forward, the ongoing recovery in the DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) chip market is the biggest reason that investors should remain interested in this stock. Generally, as the DRAM market performs, Micron's stock tends to follow suit.

The company's third quarter of FY 2025 demonstrated robust DRAM bit demand driven by AI in the data center, and Micron is shipping the chips to meet that demand. Its DRAM bit shipments grew 20% sequentially during the third quarter. A bit is a unit of digital storage capacity. Bit growth refers to the total number of memory bits that Micron manufactures and/or sells.

HBM, a specialized portion of the DRAM market, is growing even faster than the overall DRAM market. Based on second quarter figures, HBM has a run rate exceeding $6 billion. Chief Business Officer ("CBO") Sumit Sadana said on the company's third quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 earnings call, "So in terms of [FY] 2026 HBM, we do expect that HBM bit growth in '26 over 2025 calendar year will be significantly faster than the overall DRAM bit growth."

This article will discuss the market demand for DRAM and the company's inventory dynamics. It will also review the company's third-quarter FY2025 report, risks, valuation, and why I still maintain a buy recommendation for Micron.

Early stages of a DRAM memory market upcycle

Micron raised its DRAM bit demand outlook for calendar year 2025 to the high teens from mid-teens. Increasing bit demand growth indicates that the market is consuming more memory, a sign of growing demand. When Micron ships more DRAM bits to meet increased demand, the increased volume can drive its total revenue higher, even if the price per bit declines slightly. Additionally, suppose the bit demand skyrockets and outstrips manufacturers' ability to supply the bits. In that case, manufacturers gain leverage to maintain or even increase Average Selling Prices (ASPs), leading to higher gross margins. Investors view increased bit demand as a positive sign for the memory industry and Micron. As demand for memory increases, the market often awards companies in the memory industry a higher valuation.

CBO Sadana said the following on the third quarter earnings call regarding demand:

The strength in our view of the AI [Artificial Intelligence] business, i.e., the data center growth, continues to be pretty robust. And our view of the AI demand is certainly supportive of the forecast that we have provided you for bit growth in calendar '25. And the other positive thing that has happened over the last 3 months is that we are seeing, after many, many quarters of a very challenging environment in the broad distribution markets, industrial markets and some parts of the automotive market, we are now starting to see really good improvements in demand in these broad distribution and industrial markets. And that is also providing a tailwind to the overall calendar '25 bit growth estimates.

The above commentary singles out the reasons behind the increased bit demand, which boil down to continued high demand from AI data centers and a rebound in areas that have been weak recently, namely, memory and storage solutions for industrial applications, memory and storage products sold for consumer electronics, which can include items like personal computers, mobile devices (smartphones, tablets), gaming systems, and other consumer-oriented devices, and memory solutions for the automotive industry.

Another positive factor on the horizon is that the company has gained leverage to increase pricing moving forward. When an analyst asked a question during the third-quarter earnings call about November's gross margins, Chief Financial Officer Mark Murphy said:

We've talked about our inventories are very lean. We're a bit constrained as we look out to that [November] quarter. So we're focused on the best pricing decisions.

"Inventories are very lean" means Micron doesn't have a lot of excess DRAM supply, putting the company in a favorable position for price negotiations with customers in a high-demand market. "We're focused on the best pricing decisions" means the company will choose to sell to customers from whom it can gain the highest price, thereby maximizing gross margins. Mark Murphy's commentary suggests that investors should expect Micron's gross margins to rise in the November quarter. If gross margins do indeed increase solidly in future quarters, the market will likely award the stock with even higher valuations.

Company fundamentals

Micron increased total revenue by 15% sequentially and 37% year-over-year to $9.3 billion, beating analysts' estimates by $448.25 million.

One of the reasons I only discussed DRAM memory recovery in the previous section and didn't discuss NAND (flash memory) is that Micron generates most of its revenue from DRAM. As the above image shows, 76% of the company's revenue comes from DRAM. I will discuss NAND recovery more in the risk section.

The sequential increase in Bit shipments by 20% in both markets indicates robust demand and may lead to a corresponding revenue increase. However, ASPs decreasing sequentially in both markets is a bad indicator for gross margins, and if ASPs decline enough, it could offset the benefits to revenue from increased bit shipments.

Micron reports GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) and non-GAAP metrics. However, it emphasizes non-GAAP metrics in its investor presentation to highlight its core profitability. Non-GAAP metrics exclude stock-based compensation ("SBC"), gains and losses from settlements, gains and losses from debt prepayments, restructuring and asset impairments, patent license charges, and the estimated tax effects of these items.

The company has a quarterly and annual SBC as a percentage of revenue of 2.72% and 3.32%, respectively, which is relatively low.

Micron's non-GAAP gross margin increased by 11 points year-over-year to 39%. Its non-GAAP operating expense (OpEx) increased by 12.70% year-over-year to $1.1 billion. Revenue growing faster than OpEx suggests that operating margins should expand rapidly. The company's third quarter FY 2025 non-GAAP operating margins expanded by nine points to 23.65%. Micron's non-GAAP diluted earnings per share ("EPS") increased by 208% year-over-year to $1.91, beating analysts' estimates by $0.31.

The company's trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash from operations ("CFO") to sales is 44.95%, meaning that for every $1 of sales over the last year, the company generated $0.45.

Micron's third quarter FY 2025 TTM CFO was $15.20 billion. Its third quarter FY 2025 TTM capital expenditure ("CapEx") increased 98% year-over-year to $13.32 billion. Generally, when a chip manufacturer increases CapEx, it means it has become confident in future demand and has increased investment to meet that demand. An increase in CapEx usually precedes future revenue growth. The company's third quarter FY 2025 TTM free cash flow ("FCF") increased from -$1.376 billion in the third quarter of 2024 to $1.881 billion. Micron's third-quarter FY 2025 TTM FCF margin is 5.6%, representing a sequential expansion of 3.7 points.

The company's third quarter FY 2025 CFO increased 84% year over year to $4.6 billion. Its third quarter FY 2025 CapEx increased 22% year-over-year to $2.7 billion. Micron's third-quarter FY 2025 adjusted FCF increased 347% to $1.9 billion.

The company ended the third quarter of FY 2025 with $12.2 billion of cash and short-term investments. When including its untapped credit facility, the company has $15.7 billion of liquidity. Chief Financial Officer Murphy said on the third quarter FY 2025 earnings call in his prepared remarks:

During fiscal Q3, we refinanced our $900 million 2027 notes with $1.75 billion in new notes maturing in fiscal years 2033 and 2036. We closed the quarter with $15.5 billion of debt, maintaining low net leverage and a weighted average debt maturity of 2032.

Micron's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.32, indicating that the company conservatively finances its operations with a higher proportion of equity than debt. Its debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 0.93, indicating that the company can repay its debt without stress.

Management's forecast for fourth-quarter FY 2025 revenue is $10.7 billion, with a range of $300 million, exceeding analysts' estimates of $9.89 billion before the earnings announcement. Following the earnings announcement, analysts raised their estimates to $10.73 billion.

If the company reaches its fourth-quarter FY gross margin forecast of 42%, its gross margins will expand by 5.5 points. Suppose the company reaches its diluted EPS of $2.50; its diluted EPS will grow 112% year over year.

This outlook is overall favorable. Since DRAM is a higher-margin business for Micron, revenue weighted toward DRAM is beneficial. Although operating expenses are increasing, investors are likely to tolerate this increase because it is primarily due to increased investment in R&D (research and development) for future technology nodes and HBM. This type of spending is necessary for long-term growth, innovation, and maintaining a competitive edge in high-growth areas, such as HBM. As long as increased OpEx is not heavily weighted towards G&A (general and administrative) increases, which mostly go to overhead, investors will generally tolerate the OpEx increase.

"Tight DRAM inventories" and "significantly reduced NAND inventories" are very favorable indicators. When Micron exhibits low or reduced inventories in a high-demand market, it means that robust demand is surpassing the company's ability to supply the market and strengthening management's ability to negotiate higher prices for sales of its DRAM and NAND products. Higher prices translate to higher revenue and gross margin.

Valuation

Micron's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 4.049, which is above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, indicating a potential sign of overvaluation.

Micron's Enterprise Value ("EV") to EBITDA ratio is 8.446, below its three-year median of 10.33 (the bull case), but higher than its five-, seven-, and ten-year medians (the bear case).

In my previous article, the stock traded below all its EV/EBITDA medians. I only calculated where it would trade at its three-year median at the time, as that figure represented the most optimistic scenario. In the calculated bull case scenario from April, I obtained an EV of $151.851 billion and a stock price of $129.73, which was 88.5% above Micron's March 18 closing price of $68.80 and remained above the closing price on July 1 of $120.89.

When I recalculated the bull case of where the EV and stock price would be today if it traded at its three-year EV-to-EBITDA median using the figures the company reported as of the close of its fiscal third quarter on May 29, 2025, I derived an EV of $161.40 billion and a stock price of $144.36, which is a 19.41% rise above its July 1 closing price. The five-, seven-, and ten-year medians are now the bear case. If it were to sell at the extreme bear case, a ten-year EV/EBITDA of 6.034, it would have an EV of $91.76 billion and a stock price of $82.07, representing a 32% decline from its July 1 closing price.

Micron's TTM GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 21.98, below the Information Technology sector median of 29.21, a potential sign of undervaluation.

One reason the stock price may have risen recently is that the market had not priced in an estimated EPS growth rate of over 500% in FY 2025. Even after the recent price move upwards, the company has a 2025 forward P/E to growth ("PEG") ratio of 0.03, calculated by dividing the 2025 forward P/E of 15.45 by an estimated EPS growth rate of 501.91%. Generally, the market considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of an undervalued stock.

Micron's FY 2026 forward PEG ratio is 0.185. Suppose Micron sold at a forward FY 2026 PEG of 1.0, its stock price would be $650.45, a 438% rise above the July 1 stock price. Micron has tremendous upside potential. However, the market is unlikely to award the stock a PEG ratio of 1.0 because of the cyclicality of the memory business. When forecasting the future of the memory business, it's best to use a pencil rather than a pen because the weather forecast can quickly change from bright and sunny to heavy rain in an instant.

Micron's price-to-FCF (P/FCF) is 73.63, well above its five-year median of 9.454. In my last article about Micron, I stated:

Usually, when a stock's P/FCF sells above its median, the market considers it overvalued. However, interpreting the P/FCF of companies in a cyclical industry requires context. When a cyclical company is peaking, or investors expect revenue growth and FCF to slow soon, a high P/FCF or a P/FCF above its median could signal overvaluation. However, suppose a cyclical company is in the early stages of a recovery. In that case, the market may undervalue the FCF growth that the company will generate as demand for its products or services increases. Micron may be in the early stages of a recovery in the memory cycle, with rising demand for HBM chips driven by AI adoption serving as a significant catalyst for FCF growth. If FCF grows faster than the stock price, its P/FCF could drop below its median, and the stock could quickly go from looking overvalued to undervalued. In my opinion, the market underestimates the company's potential FCF growth.

At the time of my previous article, the company's P/FCF was 128.52. The P/FCF decline to 73.63 signals that Micron's FCF is growing faster than its stock price. If the company is indeed in the early stages of a memory cycle recovery, its P/FCF may continue down each quarter as the stock grows into its valuation.

Risks

Although the company relies less on the NAND market, it's still an essential component of Micron's business. Although the NAND market exhibits decent growth, its market dynamics are less healthy than those in the DRAM market. Chief Financial Officer Murphy said on the third quarter FY 2025 earnings call:

The NAND volumes were very strong. So we're benefiting from some more loading there. But still, we're very careful about capacity in that market because that part of the business, the market environment is challenging. So again, we talk about low CapEx levels, careful about node transition, where our bits are going and premium products in that market and watching inventories carefully. Inventories improved in NAND actually in the quarter, but they remain less healthy than DRAM, which DRAM, we actually expect to be below target by the end of the year.

Micron must carefully manage the amount of NAND it supplies to the market, as oversupplying the market risks lowering the prices it can charge its customers. In other words, while DRAM is firmly in recovery, the NAND market is in much earlier stages of recovery, which could quickly reverse if Micron and other NAND suppliers don't carefully manage their inventories.

The risk of oversupply and pricing pressure in the NAND segment remains. If pricing in the NAND market collapses, it would be detrimental to Micron. The company's overall gross margins could decline, even if its DRAM business continues to do well. Additionally, collapsing NAND prices may force the company to write down its inventories, as unsold inventory could rapidly become obsolete. Inventory write-downs would adversely impact its operating margins. Investors would likely notice the declining profitability and revalue the stock accordingly.

Although the DRAM market is currently in an upturn, the memory market is highly cyclical, meaning upturns can quickly turn into downturns. For instance, a downturn in the global economy could quickly alter the dynamics of the DRAM memory market to an unfavorable one. Additionally, if DRAM memory manufacturers become undisciplined and expand too aggressively, prices could erode, leading to a decline in profitability. Investors follow industry dynamics, and once they observe the declining health of the DRAM market, they will quickly sell off the stock.

Micron mentions in its guidance that it doesn't include the impact of any potential future tariffs. CBO Sumit Sadana noted in the earnings call, "there is uncertainty related to tariffs and whatever may hence happen to the macro environment." Although the U.S. has made some progress in bilateral trade negotiations with certain countries, there remains potential for tariffs on others that could harm Micron's business by disrupting supply chains, altering customer purchasing patterns, and increasing manufacturing or shipping costs.

Micron Remains a Buy

This stock is not for everyone. Risk-averse investors may want to avoid investing in it due to uncertainty about how long the memory bull market will last and how much upside Micron will achieve from a favorable market. However, investors seeking a potentially high-return stock over the short term may consider this investment. To give investors an idea of the potential upside in a DRAM bull market, let's examine the period when the DRAM market experienced an upturn at the beginning of 2020, which continued through the end of 2021 or the beginning of 2022. Micron's closing price on March 16, 2020, was $33.70. The stock reached an intraday price of $96.75 on December 29, 2021, representing a 187% increase in less than two years. So, Micron's stock price can rise substantially in a DRAM bull market.

Some believe that the current DRAM bull market can last two years or more, driven by the extraordinarily high demand from AI data centers. Analysts' revenue and EPS estimates imply that the current memory market will last until FY 2027. Barring a dramatic decline in the global economy, that assessment of the bull market's length may be correct. If the DRAM bull market lasts for an additional two years, I believe investors who purchase now could achieve more than 100% upside over the next two years. Micron is a buy for risk-tolerant investors looking for a high-return investment over the next one to two years.

data.title: A Key Pattern Is Forming In Silver (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

Although silver has been quiet in recent weeks, leaving investors puzzled, a bullish chart pattern now appears to be taking shape - indicating that the rally may soon resume.

For context, both gold and silver have been in healthy sideways consolidations, taking a breather after strong runs earlier this year. This kind of pause is typical in financial markets, especially during the quieter summer months when trading activity slows as traders head off on vacation. Let’s take a closer look at the charts.

About a month ago, silver finally broke out above two key resistance zones that had capped its gains for the past year: first the $32–$33 zone, and then the $34–$35 zone. This breakout is a strong indication that silver’s bull market is gaining momentum.

While silver has spent the past few weeks in a healthy consolidation, it has managed to hold onto its gains — a very constructive sign. I’m now watching for a breakout from this consolidation, which I believe will set the stage for a rapid move to $40 an ounce and beyond.

Interestingly, if you take a closer look, the recent consolidation actually resembles a classic bull flag pattern — exactly what you want to see in a strong uptrend. This pattern indicates further gains are likely on the horizon, though a decisive breakout to the upside with strong volume is needed for confirmation.

It’s also worth noting that silver often rallies in this step-like fashion, moving from one technical pattern to the next.

For example, last month’s $4.50 per ounce surge was preceded by a well-defined triangle pattern that formed in April and May. The current bull flag appears to be continuing this pattern of behavior, making it an exciting setup to watch for us silver bulls.

In addition to tracking COMEX silver futures, I also closely monitor silver priced in euros. This helps strip out the influence of U.S. dollar fluctuations and often provides a clearer view of the underlying trend. Euro-denominated silver also tends to respect key €1 increments—such as €30, €31, and €32—which often act as support and resistance levels.

Similar to COMEX silver futures, silver priced in euros has struggled with two major resistance zones: €29–€30 and €31–€32. Recently, it managed to break through the €29–€30 level, but its first attempt to clear €31–€32 was short-lived, as silver retreated in tandem with a strengthening euro.

The encouraging news is that silver is once again pushing into the €31–€32 zone and looks poised for another breakout attempt — especially if U.S. dollar-priced silver futures (as shown in the earlier charts) break out of its bull flag pattern. Ideally, I’d like to see a confirmed breakout in euros as well, which would signal that silver’s bull market is broad-based and not merely a result of recent dollar weakness.

I’ve also developed a proprietary indicator called the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI), designed to help validate silver’s price action and filter out potential false breakouts.

The SSPI is calculated as the average of gold and copper prices, with copper scaled by a factor of 540 to prevent gold from dominating the index. Interestingly, even though silver isn’t part of the calculation, the SSPI closely tracks its movements.

Excitingly, the SSPI finally broke out of its 2,800–3,000 trading range over the past few days, where it had been stuck since March — a promising sign for silver.

This breakout suggests a new tailwind for silver, as strong performances in both gold and copper tend to put pressure on arbitrage trading algorithms to buy silver in sympathy.

The move in the SSPI was one of the key confirmations I had been watching for, along with a breakout in silver priced in euros, which I’m still waiting to see.

The consolidation — or bull flag — pattern forming in silver is also clearly visible in silver mining stocks, as reflected in popular exchange-traded funds (ETFs) like SIL, which tracks large silver miners.

A decisive breakout from silver’s bull flag, combined with a high-volume breakout in SIL, is likely to ignite a powerful rally in the silver mining sector. I personally invest in both silver bullion and select mining stocks.

Another key asset I closely monitor to better understand the broader commodity landscape is the U.S. dollar—specifically the U.S. Dollar Index, which has historically moved inversely to commodities, including precious metals. As I’ve recently noted, the Dollar Index broke below the critical 100 support level, which has now flipped into resistance.

This marks a significant technical breakdown that tilts the outlook for the dollar decidedly bearish—while reinforcing a strong bullish backdrop for commodities like gold, silver, platinum, and copper.

Notably, the dollar’s weakness amid recent Middle East turmoil—an environment that typically strengthens safe-haven assets like the dollar—signals deeper underlying fragility.

To summarize, I’m encouraged by the bull flag pattern forming in silver right now, as this classic continuation pattern indicates even further upside ahead — potentially mirroring last month’s sharp $4.50-per-ounce surge in just a few days.

Of course, I always wait for confirmation through a decisive breakout on strong trading volume, which is what I’m watching for now. I’m feeling optimistic and, as always, I’ll keep you all updated.
